speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 3, [BACKGROUND NOISE] September 15, 1995, interview with Gizel Berman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's just wait until the Life Flight clears. And then we'll start. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't hear it. What? I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: One minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My face. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Will you continue, now, with the time when you were forced to leave your home, when things really changed and you were displaced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from '38 on, that we were occupied by the Hungarians, everything was a different life. The people changed. People showed easily their dislike for each other. And there is a difference. And the Jewish people felt it and were scared. And it was-- it was very, very scary. But nobody was having the vaguest idea that how will it continue and how will it end up. I mean, that-- that's-- as I said, I got used to it even in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Slovak-- and then they would ask you, what's your nationality? And I would say, Slovak. [LAUGHS] The teacher would say, Jewish, [NON-ENGLISH]. Anyway, they made a big deal.

speaker: interviewee
text: But once you were married and we shared everything, the good and not good, it was-- it was beautiful. It was just beautiful because the pleasures were twice as much. And the difficulties were just half as difficult. Because it was 100% togetherness. And my mother was just great that she really made it possible. Like, she right away said, the house is huge, my child-- or children. But if you don't mind-- I don't know how it came about, but I know that we said, no, you should stay. It was a big house. And she stayed. And then Nick's office came to the house. And it was big change-- big change all the way, but definitely for the better. Everybody was happier. Except the political scene was very, very, very dark. And then when the-- Nick's-- one of the-- one of the prominent patients of Nick, the governor, really must have liked him. Because he wanted, just on our own, without asking, without being asked or something, to help him. And he's the one who told to Nick that, look, I want you and your family to go up in the mountains as a certain kind of physician. And I want you to be there. And it's-- would be a good idea. But we never dreamed why. But once we left, we realized, just very quickly, that this was definitely a lifesaving suggestion or plan that-- to send us up there because the ghetto got organized, or started to function, like, three days after we left. And they started to carry in the people and amongst the first was my grandfather. And my mother was besides herself. And yes, we did agree that we would go up to that place in the hills-- I should say, hills. It's like here, the Appalachian, kind of similar. And we had no idea. We had to go with an ox cart. We knew it would be as primitive as it could be. But Nick and I felt if the governor is trying to give us this chance, you shouldn't just take it lightly. And my mother said, no way, she wouldn't come with us. And she-- and my brother was married, meantime, my older brother. That's right. He was married, and they had a child. So my mother was, of course, involved also with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, anyway, we went up to the hill country. And supposedly, it was an epidemic of thyroid or typhus-- thyroid? No, no, thyroid is not-- Typhoid.

speaker: interviewee
text: Typhoid fever. And we had some very meager accommodations. Well, anyway, we took with us some cash money. We took some jewelry with us. I don't even know why. But we did because, at that point, everywhere is afraid. You never know when you are being picked up someplace and just sent off. And so we arrived up in the mountainous place. And we opened up one of the wood slots on the one of the rooms. And we put it in-- just in newspaper-- and the money and some of the gold watches and some gold. And we felt good that we have it there. And life was just no life. It was just unreal. It was just-- there was nothing but tension, waiting. And from day one, we were being told they were taking in all the Jews from the countryside, too, to the ghetto. And, meantime, my-- because my grandfather was taken in to the ghetto, my mother tried to commit suicide. And she ended up in the hospital. They saved her. And she was there until the day we got to the ghetto because she was badly hurt with the suicidal attempt. But she survived it. And then found my grandfather and Nick's father. They were all the same age. And they took these older people earlier because they made a whole transport just of the old people. They sent them directly with those cattle cars to the crematorium, just no, no, no pretension. By the time we were in there, then it was still a pretension. We are going to work and this. And we should take working clothes and everything. But no, it wasn't that. And so we didn't stay very long up in the mountain because I felt that-- terrible that my mother is in a hospital. And the same time, the nurse was the best liaison, Nick's nurse. She was a wonderful person, [NON-ENGLISH]. And she kept in touch with us and informed everything. And Nick and I, we both realized, we don't want to save just ourselves, and everybody else will be killed or taken away someplace. So the amazing thing was that same day that we already decided that next day, we shall just go to report that we want to go into the city, in the evening-- I don't know if Nick mentioned it that the farmers, who wanted to-- which was a very moving experience. They wanted to help us. But we just took one look at each other, and we knew. We don't want to be saved. We just want to be with our family. And next day, we did go in with the-- we went in with a-- on the way down, we rented a horse, a horse and buggy somehow, not an ox cart. And we actually had a smile on our face from the beginning to the end because we shared it together with our family. And once we got into the ghetto, it was very, very, very, shocking, very different. But the fact that people were together, there was a very comforting feeling. So we just kept thinking of the positive. Well, look, we share it together with our family. And whatever will happen, it's-- we shall be together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was thinking of my older brother by that time and the child-- anyway, the ghetto was a nightmare of nightmares because there was-- nobody tried to pretend, or could pretend. Everybody suffered from right and left. And the whole atmosphere was, so, so what's next? So next was just that they will take us away. But we still didn't think they'd take us away to kill us. We just thought they'd take us away to work. We knew Germany is under lots of stress. It needs people. But we still didn't think that this is the solution. Did you have any information about what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody had the vaguest idea-- nothing whatsoever. Nothing. The kind--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long-- oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, the way we chose the clothes for warm weather to work, for hot weather, to cold weather to work, and we just really were-- the way I remember, we were really expected to go someplace-- we didn't know where-- and just to work. Give our energy and strength we have for the Germans, so they can keep on fighting. And will be what will be. But that's what we shall do. And if we shall work, they will feed us. And when it's over, the war, shall be all together. It was kind of a very simplistic way to think. But nobody could dream of what it would happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there? We were-- we were for about three weeks in the ghetto. And that was pretty tough there. But, anyway, then they took us all-- no, I mean, we filled up a cattle car. And before we went into the cattle car, a little canvas, little tepee-- no, hut was fixed up. And Hungarian soldiers insisted the women have to come there and undress. And they will examine if they are not smuggling something out-- and just real, real sadistic, horrible cruelty for no reason at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you examined?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, everybody, all the women. So what could you take out, especially in your vagina? What could you take out? But that was-- that was the most horrible thing, that these are not the enemies. These are so-called Hungarians. And we spoke the language and everything. It didn't matter. They were just horrible. But, as I said, we got used to it. And that's the way it goes. And after, once we were in the ghetto, we were just hoping to be taken away, because we thought the minute we are working someplace, we shall be really treated differently. Well, that didn't work out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they took us away and we went in that cattle car, and I told to Nick that he should be in charge of the cattle car, so he can keep his wristwatch-- he told you, I'm sure, about the wristwatch-- that was a horrible story with him, horrible. Just horrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, at the same time, the fact that we had poison with us, that was a very comforting feeling. We had poison for Nick, myself, and for my mother. But I had a cuckoo girlfriend who didn't want to go with her own family. She just wanted to come with mine. And she was just sitting next-- we were just sitting on top of each other like herrings, like sardines in a box or something. And at one point, when Nick was taken away because of the wristwatch argument, something, and about three or four hours went by and he didn't come back, I just decided, I said, gee, for my mother to kill herself, it would be no big deal. She tried already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's only me. I don't want to live. I just-- no. So I tried to whisper to my mother, who was as close as this [POINTS] handle, that what I want to do. But I did barely finish one third of my sentence and my girlfriend got hysterical. She gave out a huge scream, scream-- hysterical. And then that shook me up just fine that I'm insane. Why would I try, even to do it here in the train? I can do it later someplace else. Why should I do it? Everybody was old. We were the so-called promise, the-- Nick and I and then a couple of other young people. We shall work hard and shall-- the old ones just can live on it, that we shall work. So I was very much at ease and relaxed, right away. Once I decided I can do it another time, that stayed with me for the rest of my life. I still have prepared-- and Nick. And we still can do it to go to sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I love the feeling. And I still can do it some other time. So I don't have to do it right now if I'm unhappy. Anyway-- and they opened the door on the next station. And Nick came back. And we didn't kill ourselves. And boy, was that a lesson.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was-- the whole atmosphere in the train-- you know, how can people from one out to the other be so dehumanizing? They give a-- what did they give you-- a bucket, men, women-- no, two buckets in one corner. No, I think just one bucket. One bucket for drinking water, and one bucket that you use for the bathroom facility.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you had to do it, you had to do it. It was the most humiliating thing I could imagine. Because, you know, I can see they want to torture you and hit you. And they hit us plenty all the way. But these other little things, it's just beyond. Nobody can explain what they went through how they felt through this dehumanization.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, when we arrived to Auschwitz, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by that time, we were not so hopeful. Because the way the SS talked to us, because when-- oh, he asked me-- one of them asked me for a hat. I should give them a hat. And I said, I can't. I have just one hat with me. I need hat, OK? He said, you won't need it. They'll shoot you the minute you get there. What do you need a hat? So I said, listen, you say what you say. I say what I say. And I was behind the bar. And he was outside the bar. But it all sunk in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, I, I just-- but nothing mattered by that time too much. By that time, it didn't because so many things went on in the ghetto, it's beyond words. You know, the shrieking in pain, the people were interrogated and beaten and tortured. And it was-- [SIGH]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when you got off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I see? A mob scene. Hundreds or thousands-- no, it was thousands of people. An idiotic welcome sign, that Arbeit macht frei, that 'work will make you free.' 'Free' is in English, yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. And it-- nothing was normal, just like, just like something-- a real surrealistic madhouse. They told the doctors to step down right away. And Nick went off first. And then they started to scream that you shouldn't worry. All the luggage would be taken care. We should just get off and just listen to the orders and what they are telling us. And with the right and to the left, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: In two minute's time, I knew it's, it's, it's, it's, it's, it's, unreal. It's just unreal. And there was just no way to hope, no way to have anything, just wonder how will it end up, and when, and which way, the killing there? And those SSes calling and pushing. It was just-- Did you say anything to Nick before you parted ways?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was nothing. It was just everything kind of like, boom, boom. The, the head of the train stepped out, or stepped down. And I think, for some reason, doctors take your medical cabinets with you, something. That's the way I remember. But I never saw Nick again. He went down. And he stood there and, and just-- make-- of course, all the other people, they were either relatives or friends. And they were all crying. And they were all asking and addressing themselves to us, you know. And already, then I started, you know, to say, don't worry. We, we, we, we shall help. We shall help. Don't worry. We shall help. We shall-- well, it never came to any help, nothing but just horrible deception. And then we were standing in line, and I was separated from my mother. And, and they were taking us for delousing. That's where I learned that word so good. It was-- that's where it was used all the time, delousing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when my mother did some delousing on me, I, I realized this is the expression for it, from, from this experience. And it was a-- just all I know, my girlfriend stayed with me next to me. And I don't remember anybody-- I think maybe Nick's sister-in-law tried to stay with me. I think she did, somehow, close by. But it was just a madhouse. And in no time, there was a Polish girl who was working there for a long time. And she says, listen. Shut up. And don't do this. Don't do that. Can you smell it? Yeah, now, that's where your mother is burning maybe already, because-- What could you smell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Burning skin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know it was that at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: But once she said it, I believed it. It wasn't normal, what the smell this was, really raw skin. Anyway-- and I kept thinking of the cold and the poison. And it didn't do me any good because that was taken away right away. And I just don't even-- I can't remember what were my thoughts. I think first, I was terribly, terribly shook up-- very, very. But once I realized that actually, people are being killed, who went to the other side. And I don't know why my feeling seems, if I think back-- I'm-- I'd rather know that my mother is dead than to go through what we are going through. This I remember. And that helped me a lot, that I just knew to-- to have gone through the humiliation. When the women started to undress, and the nudity-- I still can't stand nude people, I mean, especially nude women.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went to a public swimming pool here because the other one was closed-- doesn't matter. And there was a lady in a wheelchair. It's first time here on Mercer Island. And she was sitting there. And out of the blue sky, she says, please, could you help me? I said sure, why?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so she has a wheelchair. She said, I will stand up in the wheelchair. But you should push-- pull off my bathing suit from my body. But you have to pull it very hard. It's very tight on me. I tell you, I was dying through that whole experience. I just was-- I can't tell you. I didn't go back anymore. I realized I just should stay away from nude bodies. And here this was a handicapped. And I did what she asked me. And then she asked me something else, but, I realized--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after-- in Auschwitz, after they sent you through the line? You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I realized that I don't have my sister, I don't have my mother, and I don't have hardly anyone. My little girlfriend was with me there. And I think, maybe, yes, the-- Nick's-- one of the sister-in-laws was with me. And then it was just a matter of following instructions. Do this, do that. And that's when it was the undressing and the shaving. And when the shaving-- I don't know. This Polish girl was probably good or bad, I don't know. But she just gave it to us right away. Don't kid yourself. Everyone who stepped over there, they are dead. They're taking them right away. You'll be dead too. So don't kid yourself. You'll be the same way in, in no time. And I kept thinking all this I might remember-- I had four fingernails beaten off in the ghetto. Once when the-- Nick--

speaker: interviewer
text: Nick told us.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So I, I had this bandage. So it was hurting me terribly. And so the only thing I was asking the Polish girl, I said, tell me, where can I see a doctor to get some medication, something for my hand. She said, idiot, shut up! Don't talk like this. They'll take you right away, and they'll burn you too, throw you in the crematory. She says, just don't even think about this hand. Just forget it. Otherwise you'll never see tomorrow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I realized that you don't ask for doctors. You don't ask for help. And you just shut up. And that was the best advice she could give. And eventually, what I know, I ended up some place on the ground, in a-- in a big cabin? Cabin? Yeah. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a cabin. And it was just a horrible sight. It just-- you see, these are the things-- it's one thing to experience it and see it and live through it. And it's another thing to try to describe it. And that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to line up? Can you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. [DRINKS] Real soon, we were lining up, taking us to another place. But the whole thing was, that it just-- it just was so chaotic. There, there was nothing to-- you, you, you, you-- your mind didn't work. You were just like, they hit you so strong on your head that you are not normal because nothing around you was normal. So you couldn't think normal. And what happened, as I said, it was such a rush of so many people. And they pushed me away. So I never had a number put on my arm because for some reason, there wasn't time because more people were coming so fast in. So they just pushed you-- get out of the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's happened. But we-- after, we got the clothes. After, we went through the showering-- no, first the shaving. And at the shaving, people were screaming, they were-- you look horrible. You look horrible. Don't tell me. Do I look horrible? Yes. So do you. You don't know. And oh god, what did it do? I don't know. Nothing was helped or done. But people just screamed from anger, from whatever. Don't think I look horrible, you look horrible. And just-- it was-- oh. I don't ever want to see naked women. How long were you in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very short-- three days. It was unbelievable. And that's why I never got a place to lie down. All I remember, the first horrible experience, that once we were there, they made us sign-- line up. And then we all got a purple bowl. And that was the only possession for the rest of the year, this purple metal bowl. And they gave us some-- they filled us up. And they said, now, eat it. Well, I couldn't see how to arrange it. I tasted it, and I thought, I'll die. Well, I figured I won't die. So I have to find another solution. And I remember, I sat down in a-- kind of in a quieter place somehow. And I had a dress on. I pulled the dress up, put my legs kind of apart, and kind of tried to camouflage it with the two hands-- I was scraping a hole in the ground, in a very dry, dry ground. And that's what I tried to accomplish. Because they say, if they catch you, you do something with the food, they shot you on the place. I don't know why that I wasn't yet ready to die that-- when they said it. But meantime, the fact was that I tried to pour that soup kind of underneath myself and between my legs to camouflage it in the ground. Well, some of the soup went there, but all of it didn't go. And I don't remember exactly-- I could hardly wait to get up. And I don't even know when I started to eat. But I know I couldn't eat that first soup, the first dinner, first supper, or something. And then we were-- then I started right away to look at more pathetic people than I was. I saw a girlfriend, yeah. That's one of the ones with the nylon stockings bet. And she was dead in a corner, just all curled up, just like no life in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I go up to her. And I say, Vera, Vera. And she looks up at me. And all I got to say, come on, try to get up. Say, what for? I can't. Why do you smile? I said, Vera, I mean, come on. We, we are not dead yet. We might be in an hour or in two hours. But we are not dead yet. Why don't you to come and hold onto me, and we shall walk together?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, not me. I don't make a step away from here. And she must have died right there in-- I don't know. Or they took her right away from there. But she just gave up. That was the whole thing. She just gave up. And I didn't give up. I knew I'll die any minute, but I didn't give up. Because meantime, I kept looking at people who could be-- and including my girlfriend, she-- I kept saying, [NON-ENGLISH] shall die. But meantime, come on, let's see-- maybe an older woman to help her to move or pull her or push her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that helped me. So I realized that from the first hour on, I tried to utilize my existing energy-- what can I do for somebody else? That, that saved me, actually. Because from then on, whatever happened, I chose the one that I can maybe help somebody, which, which I did, which I did, all the way through, all the way through.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lady cried to me that somebody's trying to take her shoes off in the night, like next day. And she doesn't know what to do if they take her shoes away. She'd be completely, just-- she has nothing. And it's-- and she just cried real bitterly. She was from our city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, you know what? I'll come, and I will sleep next to you. And I went and lied-- sleep. I mean it's like, take one animal and another animal in a-- in a barn. I mean, that was, what was it? That's where we were, in some kind of a barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I waited. And sure enough, somebody starts to come up to her, in the night. All I had with me is my red dish, my red metal dish, my eating [? piece. ?] That-- always, wherever we went, we took it with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what can I do for this woman? So I started to hit her with my red metal dish. And, and, and we're--
